title: Līlāvatī
author: Bhāskara II (Bhāskarācārya)
summary: The Līlāvatī is the arithmetic-and-geometry section of Bhāskara II's larger astronomical compendium Siddhāntaśiromaṇi. In thirteen chapters it treats arithmetic operations, fractions, proportions, arithmetic and geometric progressions, interest, plane and solid mensuration, gnomon/shadow problems, indeterminate equations (kuṭṭaka), and combinatorics, often framed as charming problems addressed to a girl named Līlāvatī. It is one of the most influential pedagogical mathematics texts of premodern India.
history: Composed by Bhāskara II (b. 1114 CE) of the Ujjain astronomical school, the work circulated for centuries as a standard arithmetic primer and was translated into Persian under Akbar (by Faizi, 1587) and later into European languages. Numerous Sanskrit commentaries (e.g. by Gaṇeśa Daivajña) attest to its enduring authority.

title: Sūryasiddhānta
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to a revelation by Sūrya, the Sun-god, to the asura Maya)
date_estimate: extant recension c. 4th-9th c. CE (composite, with earlier and later layers); core often placed c. 400-600 CE
summary: The Sūryasiddhānta is one of the foundational Sanskrit treatises of Indian mathematical astronomy, presented as instruction revealed by the Sun-god. In fourteen chapters it covers planetary mean and true motions, eclipses, heliacal risings and settings, the phases of the moon, planetary conjunctions, cosmography, time-reckoning, and the construction of astronomical instruments, and it embeds early sine-table trigonometry.
history: The text is a 'living document' revised over centuries; the surviving version differs from the older Sūryasiddhānta paraphrased by Varāhamihira in his Pañcasiddhāntikā. It became the most widely cited and commented astronomical authority in India and underlies much of later Hindu calendrical practice; E. Burgess produced an influential English translation in 1860.

title: Tikanikayātrā
author: Varāhamihira
summary: The Tikanikayātrā is one of Varāhamihira's shorter 'yātrā' (military expedition / journey) astrology works, concerned with determining auspicious times and omens for a king setting out on a campaign or journey. It treats planetary and nakṣatra configurations, omens, and ritual precautions governing the success of an expedition, condensing the subject matter handled at greater length in his Bṛhadyātrā and Yogayātrā.
history: Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE), the celebrated astronomer-astrologer of Ujjain, composed several works on yātrā (catarchic/military astrology); the Tikanikayātrā is the most compact of these and survives mainly through manuscripts and editions of his astrological corpus.

title: Vivāhapaṭala
author: Varāhamihira
summary: The Vivāhapaṭala ('chapter/section on marriage') is Varāhamihira's specialized treatise on the astrology of marriage: determining auspicious times (muhūrta) for weddings, examining the compatibility of bride and groom through nakṣatra and planetary factors, and the omens and configurations bearing on conjugal life. It systematizes the kūṭa-based compatibility analysis that became standard in later Hindu matchmaking.
history: Ascribed to Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE) of Ujjain, the Vivāhapaṭala is counted among his astrological works and survives as a short specialized tract; some manuscripts treat marriage-electional material that overlaps with chapters of his larger compendia.

title: Yogayātrā
author: Varāhamihira
summary: The Yogayātrā is Varāhamihira's treatise on military and journey astrology, in roughly sixteen chapters (about 485 verses), prescribing how a king should determine auspicious times, omens, planetary yogas, propitiatory rites, and precautions for setting out on a military campaign. It blends astrological calculation with omen-lore (śakuna) and ritual.
history: Composed by Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE), the Yogayātrā is one of his several yātrā works alongside the larger Bṛhadyātrā and the brief Tikanikayātrā; this is one digital recension (variant '1') of the text.

title: Yogayātrā
author: Varāhamihira
summary: This is a second digital recension (variant '2') of Varāhamihira's Yogayātrā, his treatise on military and journey astrology. In about sixteen chapters and roughly 485 verses it lays out the determination of auspicious times, omens, planetary yogas, propitiatory rituals, and precautions for a king's expedition.
history: Ascribed to Varāhamihira (c. 505-587 CE) of Ujjain; this entry corresponds to an alternate manuscript/electronic version of the same Yogayātrā, distinct only in its textual recension from the parallel record.

title: Āṅgirasasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Aṅgiras)
date_estimate: uncertain; 1st millennium CE, likely later layers mid-to-late (disputed)
summary: The Āṅgirasasmṛti is one of the minor dharmaśāstra texts ascribed to the Vedic sage Aṅgiras. It deals chiefly with penance and expiation (prāyaścitta), ritual purity and impurity, śrāddha (rites for ancestors), and prescriptions varying by caste; some recensions are arranged in two parts (Pūrva- and Uttara-Āṅgiras).
history: Like other minor smṛtis it survives in varying manuscript forms and is heavily quoted in medieval dharmanibandhas (digests); its attribution to Aṅgiras is traditional rather than historical, and its date is hard to fix because the transmitted material is a layered compilation.

title: Bṛhaspatismṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); modern reconstruction by K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar
summary: The Bṛhaspatismṛti is a lost dharmaśāstra known only through quotations preserved in later commentaries and digests; scholars have reconstructed it from these citations. It is especially noted for its developed treatment of vyavahāra (judicial procedure and substantive law), expanding upon and serving as a kind of vārttika (gloss) on the Manusmṛti, and it ranks among the most juristically sophisticated smṛtis.
history: No complete manuscript survives; Julius Jolly translated some 700 vyavahāra verses (SBE 33) and K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar compiled and edited a fuller reconstruction (~2,400 verses, Gaekwad Oriental Series 85, Baroda 1941). The text closely follows and elaborates Manu, placing it after the Manusmṛti.

title: Bṛhaspatismṛti: Vyavahārakāṇḍa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); reconstructed
summary: This is the Vyavahārakāṇḍa (book on legal/judicial procedure) of the reconstructed Bṛhaspatismṛti. It treats the conduct of lawsuits, courts and judges, evidence (witnesses, documents, ordeals/divya), the eighteen titles of law (vyavahārapadas), contracts, debts, inheritance, and related substantive law, representing a high point of classical Hindu juristic thought.
history: The Vyavahārakāṇḍa is the best-preserved portion of the otherwise lost Bṛhaspatismṛti, recovered from citations; about 717 vyavahāra verses were collected and translated by Jolly (SBE 33) and the GRETIL plain-text edition reflects this reconstructed legal section.

title: Kātyāyanasmṛti [Kātyāyanasmṛtisāroddhāra]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Kātyāyana); reconstruction by P. V. Kane
date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (later than Manu and Yājñavalkya); reconstruction published 1933
summary: The Kātyāyanasmṛti is a lost dharmaśāstra, here in P. V. Kane's reconstructed 'Sāroddhāra' (essence-extraction) form, focused on vyavāhara—judicial procedure and the substantive law of inheritance, contracts, debts, partition, and women's property (strīdhana). Together with Nārada and Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana represents the high-water mark of classical smṛti jurisprudence dealing almost exclusively with law proper.
history: No independent manuscript of Kātyāyana's law text survives; P. V. Kane reconstructed about a thousand verses from quotations in commentaries and digests, publishing 'Kātyāyanasmṛti on Vyavahāra' (Bombay, 1933). The text is dated after the Manusmṛti and Yājñavalkyasmṛti.

title: Vīramitrodaya: Samayaprakāśa
author: Mitramiśra (Mitra Miśra)
genre: nibandha (dharmaśāstra digest), section on calendar/ritual time
summary: The Samayaprakāśa ('illumination of [ritual] times') is one section of Mitramiśra's vast dharmaśāstra digest Vīramitrodaya. It treats the religious calendar—the determination of festivals, vratas (vows), fasts, sacred days, and the auspicious times (samaya) for observances—drawing on the smṛtis and earlier digests to fix proper ritual timing.
history: Composed by Mitramiśra of Gopācala (Gwalior) at the behest of King Vīrasiṃhadeva of Orchhā during the Mughal period, the Vīramitrodaya closely follows the Mitākṣarā and became a leading authority of the Banaras school of Hindu law; the Samayaprakāśa was edited by Vishnu Prasāda Bhandari (Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series, Benares 1935).

title: Nāradasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Nārada)
date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; late 4th to 8th c. CE)
summary: The Nāradasmṛti is the dharmaśāstra 'juridical text par excellence,' the only major smṛti devoted purely to vyavahāra (law and legal procedure) and omitting ācāra (conduct) and prāyaścitta (penance). It systematically treats courts, the eighteen titles of law, evidence including ordeals (divya), witnesses, documents, debts, contracts, partition, and the duties of the king as administrator of justice.
history: Attributed to the divine sage Nārada, the text exists in shorter (minor/Jolly) and longer recensions; its purely procedural focus made it prized by rulers across India and Southeast Asia. Lariviere produced a critical edition and translation; its date is debated within the second half of the first millennium CE.

title: Nāradasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Nārada)
date_estimate: c. 4th-6th c. CE (disputed; late 4th to 8th c. CE)
summary: This is a second recension/digital version of the Nāradasmṛti, the dharmaśāstra concerned exclusively with vyavāhara (jurisprudence). It covers judicial procedure, the eighteen titles of law, modes of proof and ordeals, debts, contracts, inheritance, and the king's judicial role, omitting personal conduct and penance.
history: Corresponds to a distinct manuscript recension (e.g. the longer/vulgate as opposed to the minor text) of the Nāradasmṛti; the two electronic records reflect different transmitted versions of the same juridical smṛti, which survives in markedly varying lengths.

title: Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā [Parāśarasmṛti]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Parāśara)
genre: smṛti / dharmaśāstra (lawbook for the Kali age)
date_estimate: uncertain; latter half 1st millennium CE (often c. 7th-11th c. CE for extant form; disputed)
summary: The Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā, or Parāśarasmṛti, is a dharmaśāstra in twelve chapters (about 600 verses) that presents itself as the lawbook appropriate to the Kali age (kaliyuga). It concentrates on ācāra (right conduct), the duties of the four varṇas and āśramas, ritual observances, purification, penance, and the merit of charity (dāna) as the supreme virtue for the present age; it largely omits detailed vyavāhara.
history: Traditionally each yuga has its own authoritative lawgiver, with Parāśara assigned to the Kali age. The smṛti became influential precisely as a 'Kali-yuga' code and acquired a major commentary, the Parāśaramādhavīya by Mādhavācārya (Sāyaṇa's brother, 14th c.); the date of the base text is disputed.

title: Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā [Parāśarasmṛti]: Kāṇḍas 1 & 2
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Parāśara)
genre: smṛti / dharmaśāstra (lawbook for the Kali age), opening sections
summary: This record covers the first two kāṇḍas (sections) of the Parāśarasmṛti. The opening portions set out the yuga-dharma framework—why Parāśara's code governs the Kali age—and begin the exposition of the duties of the varṇas and āśramas, daily and ritual conduct (ācāra), and the foundations of purification and penance.
history: A partial (sectional) version of the Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā; the work is the recognized Kali-yuga lawbook and received the celebrated commentary Parāśaramādhavīya by Mādhavācārya in the 14th century.

title: Mitākṣarā
author: Vijñāneśvara
summary: The Mitākṣarā is Vijñāneśvara's authoritative commentary on the Yājñavalkyasmṛti. Best known for its doctrine of 'inheritance by birth' (a son acquires an interest in ancestral property at birth) and its treatment of the joint/coparcenary family, it systematizes Hindu personal law on partition, succession, adoption, women's property, and judicial procedure.
history: Written by Vijñāneśvara at the Western Cālukya court of Vikramāditya VI at Kalyāṇa, the Mitākṣarā gave rise to the dominant 'Mitākṣarā school' of Hindu law observed across most of India outside Bengal/Assam, and remained central to Anglo-Hindu jurisprudence into the modern era. Its exact date is debated (Kane, Derrett).

title: Mitākṣarā, a commentary on the Yājñavalkya-dharmaśāstra
author: Vijñāneśvara
summary: Same work as the preceding record: Vijñāneśvara's Mitākṣarā commentary on the Yājñavalkyasmṛti (here titled explicitly as a commentary on the Yājñavalkya-dharmaśāstra). It glosses and systematically expounds Yājñavalkya's verses on conduct (ācāra), judicial procedure (vyavāhara), and penance (prāyaścitta), and is foundational for the law of inheritance by birth.
history: This is a parallel/alternate digital record of the Mitākṣarā by Vijñāneśvara, composed at the Cālukya court under Vikramāditya VI; it founded the Mitākṣarā school of Hindu law and is among the most influential legal texts of premodern India.

title: Yājñavalkyasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (disputed; some place core in 1st-2nd c. CE)
summary: The Yājñavalkyasmṛti is a major verse dharmaśāstra organized into three sections—ācāra (right conduct), vyavāhara (judicial process), and prāyaścitta (penance/expiation). More concise and systematically arranged than the Manusmṛti, it is especially esteemed for its lucid treatment of legal procedure and had a greater influence on medieval Indian judicial practice than Manu.
history: Attributed to the Upaniṣadic sage Yājñavalkya, the extant text is a later composition of the dharmaśāstra tradition. It became the base text for the most important medieval commentaries—above all Vijñāneśvara's Mitākṣarā and the Vīramitrodaya—anchoring Hindu personal law.

title: Yamasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Yama)
summary: The Yamasmṛti is one of the minor dharmaśāstra texts, ascribed to Yama (named as a lawgiver already in the Yājñavalkyasmṛti's list). It is devoted largely to penance and expiatory atonement (prāyaścitta) for sins and ritual transgressions, purification, and the rules of śrāddha—the propitiatory rites for the ancestors—along with their theoretical justification.
history: Surviving as a short text quoted extensively in medieval digests, the Yamasmṛti exists in differing recensions; its attribution to Yama is traditional, and its precise date cannot be securely fixed.

title: Laghuyamasmṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Yama)
summary: The Laghuyamasmṛti ('shorter Yama-smṛti') is a brief recension of the dharmaśāstra material ascribed to Yama, concentrating on penance and expiation (prāyaścitta), ritual purification, and śrāddha for the ancestors. It belongs to the family of minor smṛtis dealing chiefly with atonement for sins and impurities.
history: The Laghuyama text is cited by medieval authorities such as Halāyudha and Mādhavācārya, confirming its currency and the existence of multiple recensions of the Yama material; its date is uncertain.
